content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : Members of the Commission                    Date  : June 23, 1976


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Natalie West

Subject: The Geographic Jurisdiction of the Courts of Record

     As you know, AB809 amended Government Code
Section 87200 to require "judges of courts of record" to
file statements disclosing their income, interests in
real property, and investments. Thus, judges of the
Supreme Court, courts of appeal, superior courts and
municipal courts must file disclosure statements al-
though they are not subject to the disqualification
provisions of the Act.

     The issue of what constitutes the "jurisdiction"
of a court of record is important because real property
and investments are not required to be disclosed unless
there are jurisdictional contacts. "Interest in
real property" includes "any leasehold, beneficial or
ownership interest or option to acquire such interest
in real property located in the jurisdiction...." Section
82033. "Investment" is limited to interests in a business
entity if the "entity or any parent, subsidiary or other
wise related business entity has an interest in real
property in the jurisdiction, or does business or plans
to do business in the jurisdiction, or has done business
within the jurisdiction at any time during the two years
prior to the time any statement or other action is required
under this title ...." Section 82034.

     The term "jurisdiction" is in turn defined in
Section 82035 to mean "the state with respect to a state
agency" or the region in which a local government agency
has jurisdiction. However, the definitions of both
"state agency" and "local government agency," Sections
82049 and 82041, expressly exclude from their scope "any
court or any agency in the judicial branch of government."
Apparently the drafters of AB809 overlooked the need to
amend certain sections of the Act in addition to Section
87200. Consequently, the Commission must rely on other
statutes as well as policy considerations to interpret

I can see this is a very faint or nearly blank document image. The image appears to be predominantly white/light gray with only very faint marks visible at the top that could be page holes or binding marks, and some barely legible text scattered throughout that is too faint to read with confidence.

Since I cannot reliably make out the text content with certainty, I should indicate this:

[The document image is too faint or low contrast to reliably transcribe the text content. Only very faint marks and possible text shadows are visible but are not legible enough to transcribe accurately.]

Members of the Commission
June 23, 1976
Page Two

the term "jurisdiction" for disclosure by judges. The
majority of the staff believes that judges should
disclose investments and interests in real property located
in California. Thus, the term "jurisdiction" should be
interpreted to mean the entire state. Reasons for this
conclusion are set forth below.

Perhaps most importantly, the courts are the
judicial arm of the state and the scope of their function
is analogous to the executive and legislative branches.
Article 5, Section 1 of the California Constitution provides
that the supreme executive power of the state is vested
in the Governor. Article 4, Section 1 vests legislative
authority in the legislature. Article 6, Section 1 provides
that "the judicial power of this State is vested in the
Supreme Court, courts of appeal, superior court, municipal
courts, and justice courts. All except justice courts
are courts of record." The term "jurisdiction" means the
state for the Governor and members of the Legislature,
and it should be interpreted in the same way for the
judicial branch. Members of the state legislature must
disclose investments and interests in real property in
the entire state even though each legislator represents a
district that encompasses only a small part of the state.
Similarly, judges should be required to disclose investments
and interests in real property throughout the state notwith-
standing the fact that the judicial district in which
they sit is much smaller than the entire state.

The argument that members of the Legislature
participate in decisions affecting the entire state while
judges usually consider matters only within their districts
lack merit. Judges are required to interpret and apply
general laws of the state. A court may adjudicate disputes
arising anywhere in the state, as long as the parties
agree to submit to the court's jurisdiction. For example,
suppose that a superior or municipal court judge sits in
Los Angeles County and has an investment in a company
located in Sacramento County. The company could bring an
action in Los Angeles County against a defendant located
in that county. Moreover, a decision in one area may have
an indirect effect on similar disputes elsewhere even if
the particular decision is not binding on other courts.
Thus, a judge in Los Angeles County who has an interest in
real property located in Yolo County may be called upon to
decide a dispute involving issues that will affect his
investment. For these reasons, it's clear that the importance
of any court's decisions extends beyond the boundaries of
the particular judicial district in which a judge may sit.


Members of the Commission
June 23, 1976
Page Three


Other legal provisions support the conclusion
that the courts should be treated as state agencies, rather
than local agencies. Judges are elected in general elections,
not local elections. Cal. Const. Article 6, Section 16.
Judicial salaries are set by the Legislature. Article 6,
Section 19. Judges are subject to suspension and censure
by the Supreme Court on recommendation of the Commission
on Judicial Qualifications, whose members are appointed by
the Governor. Article 6, Section 18. All of these factors
indicate that judges serve as part of a statewide judicial
system which should be treated as a state agency, even if
individual judges serve in districts that encompass only a
part of the state. Consequently, the staff recommends
that the Commission interpret the term "jurisdiction" to
include the entire state for purposes of disclosure by all
judges of courts of record.


[illegible]

sections.question_synthetic: What geographic jurisdiction should be applied when judges of courts of record disclose their investments and real property interests under AB809's amendments to Government Code Section 87200?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC staff recommends interpreting 'jurisdiction' to mean the entire state of California for disclosure purposes by all judges of courts of record, rather than limiting disclosure to their specific judicial districts, because courts function as part of the statewide judicial system analogous to executive and legislative branches.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Internal FPPC memorandum to Commission members; formatted as OCR; no traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections but contains clear legal issue and staff recommendation; reasoning spans multiple pages with constitutional and statutory analysis.

embedding.summary: This memorandum addresses the geographic scope of disclosure requirements for judges under AB809, recommending that judges disclose investments and real property interests throughout California rather than only within their specific judicial districts, based on the statewide nature of the judicial system.
